organizations_and_honors.organizations_belonged_to_excluding_race:
Association for Computing Machinery (ACM), University of Washington Chapter – Active member from freshman through senior year. I attended weekly meetings, helped organize the annual hackathon (DubHacks) by recruiting sponsors and mentoring first-time participants, and co-led a study group for the introductory programming series. I also served as the logistics coordinator during my senior year, which meant coordinating room reservations, AV needs, and food orders for events that regularly drew 80–120 students.

Husky Coding Project (student-run software development club) – Joined sophomore year and stayed involved through graduation. I worked on a team of 6 to build a web application for a local non-profit that tracks volunteer hours and event attendance. I was responsible primarily for the backend API design and documentation, and I also ran bi-weekly code review sessions.

Data Science Society at UW – Member during junior and senior years. Participated in Kaggle-style competitions hosted on campus, where our team placed in the top 10% twice. I also gave a short workshop on introductory data visualization using Python and seaborn for about 40 students.

Graduate Data Science Association, UC Berkeley – As a master’s student, I joined this organization my first semester and later served as events chair. I helped plan panel discussions with industry data scientists from local companies, organized a monthly “Lightning Talk” series where students presented their in-progress research, and coordinated a mentorship program pairing first-year master’s students with second-year students.

Women in Computing at Berkeley (WiC@B) – Ally member and volunteer. I participated in resume review sessions and mock interview nights, acting as a peer interviewer for undergraduates preparing for internships. I also volunteered at outreach events for local high school students interested in STEM fields.

Community Tech Volunteers – A local community group in Seattle I joined after undergrad. We host free monthly digital literacy workshops at the public library, teaching older adults and recent immigrants basic computer skills like using email, creating strong passwords, and recognizing phishing attempts. I usually prepare step-by-step handouts and stay after sessions to answer one-on-one questions.
organizations_and_honors.honors_awards_or_other_forms_of_recognit:
Dean’s List, University of Washington (multiple quarters between 2016 and 2019) – Awarded for maintaining a quarterly GPA above 3.5 while enrolled full-time. This recognition appears on my transcript for 6 separate quarters. It reflects consistent academic performance across core computer science courses such as data structures, algorithms, operating systems, and databases.

Undergraduate Research Symposium Presenter, University of Washington (May 2018) – Selected to present a poster on a research project I completed under the supervision of Professor Elaine Park in the Computer Science & Engineering department. The project focused on building a machine learning model to predict student performance in introductory programming courses using early assignment and quiz data. I was responsible for data cleaning, feature engineering, and model evaluation. Presenting at the symposium helped me practice explaining technical work to a mixed audience of students, faculty, and community members.

Husky Hackathon (DubHacks) – Best Social Impact Project (October 2017) – My team of four developed a prototype mobile application that helps connect volunteers with local food banks in need of short-notice help. I implemented the REST API and integrated a basic push notification system. The judges highlighted our clear problem definition, the feasibility of deploying the solution with minimal additional resources, and the potential to partner with existing non-profits.

Outstanding Teaching Assistant Award, UC Berkeley (May 2021) – Received departmental recognition for my work as a Graduate Student Instructor (GSI) for an introductory data science course. I led two weekly discussion sections of around 30 students each, held regular office hours, and created supplemental practice problems to help students who were new to programming. Student evaluations frequently mentioned that I was approachable, gave concrete examples (such as using real-world datasets like bike-share usage and movie ratings), and helped reduce anxiety around coding assignments.

Capstone Project Recognition, UC Berkeley M.S. in Data Science (May 2022) – Our three-person capstone team received an informal recognition from faculty for producing one of the strongest projects in our cohort. We built a predictive model to help a regional healthcare network identify patients at risk of missing follow-up appointments. My role included designing the data pipeline, performing exploratory data analysis, and working closely with our faculty advisor to ensure the model outputs were interpretable to clinicians. The project culminated in a detailed report and a live demo to a panel of faculty and industry partners.

Community Volunteer Appreciation Certificate, Seattle Public Library (June 2023) – Awarded in acknowledgment of more than 40 hours of volunteer service delivering digital literacy workshops and one-on-one tech support sessions. The library staff specifically noted my patience when working with patrons who had very limited prior computer experience and my ability to break down tasks (like setting up an email account or learning how to use video calling) into manageable, confidence-building steps.
